title: Basic Principles
subtitle: "Chess is 99% tactics." — Richard Teichmann

sections.title: 2.1 The Three Golden Rules of the Opening

blocks.html:
For the first 10-15 moves of every game, you have three jobs. Memorize them:

Rule 1: Control the Center

The center squares (e4, d4, e5, d5) are the high ground. Pieces in the center attack more squares and reach both sides faster.

Good first moves: 1. e4, 1. d4, 1. Nf3, 1. c4
Bad first moves: 1. a4, 1. h4, 1. Na3

The test: After every move, ask — "Did this move increase my control over the center?"

Rule 2: Develop Your Pieces (Quickly!)

"Development" means bringing pieces off the back rank and into the game.

The ideal first 10 moves: Move both center pawns, bring out both knights, bring out both bishops, castle.

Rule of thumb: Don't move the same piece twice in the opening unless forced.

Rule 3: King Safety (Castle Early!)

Castle within the first 5-10 moves. When in doubt, castle kingside.

blocks.caption: After 4 moves in the Italian Game: White has claimed the center (e4, preparing d4), developed both knights (c3, f3), developed a bishop (c4), and is ready to castle.

blocks.html:
What NOT to Do in the Opening

MistakeWhy It's Bad
Bringing queen out earlyShe becomes a target
Moving pawns on the edge (a4, h4)Doesn't control center
Making too many pawn movesPawns don't develop
Not castlingKing sits exposed

Between ELO 200-400, most games are lost because one player simply doesn't see that their piece is being attacked.

The Pre-Move Checklist

Before you touch a piece, ask three questions:

  1. Checks: Can I check my opponent? Can my opponent check me?
  2. Captures: What can I capture? What can my opponent capture?
  3. Threats: What am I threatening? What is my opponent threatening?

This is the CCT Framework — Checks, Captures, Threats. Get in the habit of scanning for CCT on every move.

Hanging Pieces

A "hanging" piece is undefended and can be captured for free. Most decisive advantages at this level come from capturing hanging pieces — and not hanging your own.

blocks.caption: White's knight on f3 attacks Black's pawn on e5. Is the e5 pawn defended? Let's count — Black's knight on c6 defends it. 1 attacker vs 1 defender. White can't win it yet.

blocks.html:
Before capturing, count:

  1. How many of your pieces attack the target
  2. How many of your opponent's pieces defend it
  3. The value of the pieces involved

If attackers > defenders: You can safely capture.
If attackers = defenders: The exchange may be equal. Capture with the least valuable piece first.

blocks.caption: White attacks e5 with 2 pieces (Nf3 + d4 pawn). Black defends e5 with 2 pieces (Nc6 + d6 pawn). White also eyes d5. Count before you capture!

blocks.html:
Tactics are short, forcing sequences that win material or deliver checkmate. Master these four basic patterns.

The Fork

A single piece attacks two (or more) enemy pieces simultaneously. The opponent can only save one. The Knight Fork is the most dangerous because knights jump unexpectedly.

blocks.caption: White's knight on e5 FORKS Black's queen and the c7 pawn. Black can only save one. This is why you should never let a knight reach e5 unchallenged!

blocks.html:
The Pin

A piece is "pinned" when moving it would expose a more valuable piece behind it to attack.

Absolute pin: The pinned piece cannot legally move (exposes king to check).
Relative pin: The piece can move, but loses material.

blocks.caption: Black's bishop on b4 PINS White's knight on c3 to the king on e1. The knight CANNOT move — doing so would expose the king to check. This is an absolute pin.

blocks.html:
The Skewer

Like a pin, but the more valuable piece is in front. The front piece must move, exposing the piece behind to capture. Also called an X-ray attack.

blocks.caption: White rook to c8+! This SKEWERS the Black king and rook. The king MUST move out of check, and then White captures the rook on e7 for free.

blocks.html:
The Discovered Attack

Move a piece to uncover an attack from another piece behind it. Devastating when combined with check (discovered check).

blocks.caption: White plays Nxf7! The knight moves, uncovering a discovered attack from the bishop on c4 to f7. Black must deal with BOTH threats — the knight and bishop both attack f7.

blocks.html:
Back Rank Mate

The enemy king is trapped on the back rank by its own pawns. A rook or queen delivers checkmate along that rank.

blocks.caption: Re8# — Back rank checkmate! The Black king is trapped behind its own pawns on f7, g7, h7. The rook delivers checkmate from e8.

blocks.html:
Prevention: Give your king "luft" (air) by pushing a pawn in front of the castled king (h3/h6 or g3/g6) before it's too late.

Smothered Mate

A knight delivers checkmate to a king completely surrounded by its own pieces.

blocks.caption: Nf7# — Smothered mate! The Black king on g8 is surrounded by a rook on f8 and pawns on g7, h7. The knight delivers checkmate — the king has nowhere to go.

blocks.html:
Mistake 1: Copycat Chess

Mirroring the opponent's moves. The player who copies second is always one move behind.

Fix: Play your own plan.

Mistake 2: Premature Attacks

Attacking before finishing development. Your attack fizzles and they counter-attack.

Fix: Complete development, castle, then attack.

Mistake 3: Trading for No Reason

Capturing just because you can. Fix: Ask "Does this trade improve my position?"

Mistake 4: Neglecting King Safety

Leaving the king in the center past move 10. Fix: Castle early.

Mistake 5: Resigning Too Early

At 200-400 ELO, comebacks happen constantly. Fix: Never resign. Make them prove it.

checkpoint: Can you name the three opening rules? Can you spot a fork, pin, or skewer when it's on the board? Do you count attackers and defenders before capturing? If yes, proceed to Chapter 3.
summary:
PrincipleKey Takeaway
Three opening rulesCenter, develop, castle
Pre-move checklistChecks -> Captures -> Threats
CountingAttackers vs. Defenders
Basic tacticsFork, pin, skewer, discovered attack
Checkmate patternsBack rank, smothered
Common mistakesDon't mirror, don't attack undeveloped, castle early
